info.name: TrustGate
info.description: Admin API for TrustGate. Manage gateways and their registries, policies, consumers, roles and auth credentials. Set the `base_url` and `admin_token` collection variables before running. IDs captured from create responses are stored automatically into `gateway_id`, `registry_id`, `policy_id`, `consumer_id`, `role_id` and `auth_id`. Creating an `api_key` auth returns the generated key exactly once; it is captured into `consumer_api_key`. Use `E2E Flow` for inline + API key, `E2E Flow (OAuth2 JWT)` for inline + classic OAuth Bearer JWT, and `E2E Role-Based Flow (OIDC)` for roles + OIDC auth.

request.description: List the currently connected config-sync data planes (self-hosted gateways streaming config). Admin-authed.

Fetch the metrics Event stored for a playground request, keyed by the X-AG-Trace-Id header returned in the proxy response. Requires an admin Bearer token. Traces expire after a short TTL (default 10m).

Optional slug (lowercase DNS label); omitted → server generates. Tenant JWT must not send entitlements (422). Platform may stamp entitlements.tier and body tenant_id (create-for-tenant). With RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED, 409 at MaxInstances; free create inherits highest sibling tier. Ownership tenant_id is required (JWT claim or body for platform admins).

exec: pm.test('tenant cannot stamp entitlements', function () { pm.response.to.have.status(422); });

request.description: Tenant JWT must not send entitlements; only platform admins may stamp tier. Expect 422 when using a tenant-scoped admin token. Ownership tenant_id is required (JWT claim or body for platform admins).
Update gateway fields. Tenant JWT + entitlements → 422. Platform tier downgrade when count > new MaxInstances → 409 (delete excess first).

item.name: Create Azure Registry (OAuth Service Principal)
item.description: Default Azure auth mode: OAuth via service principal. Requires auth.type=azure, azure.endpoint, azure.tenant_id, azure.client_id, and azure.client_secret. It is not generic auth.type=oauth2.

item.name: Create Azure Registry (API Key)
item.description: Azure API key mode requires auth.type=azure, azure.endpoint, and azure.api_key.

item.name: Create Azure Registry (DefaultAzureCredential)
item.description: The public field use_managed_identity is retained for compatibility, but TrustGate uses Azure SDK DefaultAzureCredential semantics for this mode.

item.name: Update Azure Registry (Preserve Secret)
item.description: Use the redaction placeholder for the same Azure mode to preserve the stored api_key or client_secret during partial updates.

item.name: Test Connection (OpenAI API Key)
item.description: Tests provider credentials without creating a registry.

item.name: Test Azure Connection (API Key)
item.description: Connection testing for Azure API key auth.

item.name: Test Azure Connection (Service Principal)
item.description: Connection testing supports Azure service principal auth under auth.type=azure.

item.name: Test Azure Connection (DefaultAzureCredential)
item.description: use_managed_identity=true keeps the existing API name, but the runtime uses Azure SDK DefaultAzureCredential.

request.description: Duplicate an existing policy. No body: the copy reuses the plugin configuration (slug, settings, stages, enabled, priority, parallel) with a fresh id and an auto-generated name (suffix 2, 3, 4...). The copy has no consumer associations and is not global. Returns 201 with the new policy.

`routing_mode` is `inline` (default) or `role_based`. Inline consumers own their registries, model policies, `lb_config` (with optional `pool_alias` addressable as `pool:<alias>` in the payload) and `fallback`. LB and fallback never apply to role_based consumers.

Role-based consumers authorize through roles and authenticate via IDP or OAuth2 client credentials. They cannot hold registries, lb_config or fallback. Existing roles can be bound atomically at creation via `roles` (or attached later with POST .../consumers/:id/roles/:role_id).

Partial update. Registries are managed via attach/detach endpoints; `model_policies` here updates policies for already-bound registries. Send `lb_config`/`fallback` with `enabled: false` to clear them.

Attaches a registry to a consumer (idempotent). The body is optional: omit it (or omit `weight`) to keep the current weight; send `weight` (1-1000) to set the per-association weight used by the weighted round-robin load balancer.
Only valid for role_based consumers. Returns 409 for inline consumers.

item.name: Roles
item.description: Roles own model/MCP policies and map IDP claims to access. Create the role, attach registries, then update it with model_policies for those registries.

request.description: `model_policies` cannot be set on create: bind registries first, then update the role. `oidc_mapping` grants the role to OIDC tokens whose claims match.
`model_policies` may only reference registries already attached to the role.

item.name: Auth Credentials

exec: // The plaintext key is generated server-side and shown ONLY here.

request.description: Classic OAuth: validates Bearer JWTs from an external authorization server (JWKS or introspection). Inline consumers only.
Validates OIDC-issued JWTs; claims are matched against role oidc_mapping rules. Role-based consumers only.
Authenticates clients by TLS certificate against a CA plus CN/DNS/fingerprint allow-lists.

item.description: Gateway auth credentials. `api_key` and `oauth2` apply to inline consumers; `oidc` applies to role_based consumers. `oauth2` is classic OAuth (Bearer JWT validation via JWKS/introspection); `mtls` authenticates via client certificate.

request.description: List the available policy plugins (slugs + schemas) that can be attached to a gateway.
List the known MCP servers that can back an MCP registry.

item.name: E2E Flow
item.description: End-to-end flow that provisions a gateway with an OpenAI backend, a policy, an API-key credential and a consumer, then sends a real chat-completion through the proxy plane (:8081). Run the requests top-to-bottom (Collection Runner runs them in order). Set the `openai_api_key` variable to a real OpenAI key before running. Step 4 generates the consumer API key server-side and captures it into `consumer_api_key`; step 5a binds the OpenAI registry with model policies at create time; step 6 sends the key in the fixed `X-AG-API-Key` header (no `X-Gateway-Id` is used).

request.description: Optional `slug` (lowercase DNS label): when omitted the server generates a unique random slug. The slug identifies the gateway on the proxy plane: `X-AG-Gateway-Slug` header (GATEWAY_DISCOVERY_MODE=header, default) or `{slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN>` host (subdomain mode). Ownership tenant_id is required (JWT claim or body for platform admins).

item.name: 2b. Create Registry (Google)
item.description: Optional provider payload. Does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

item.name: 2c. Create Registry (Vertex)
item.description: Optional provider payload. Does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

item.name: 2d. Create Registry (Anthropic)
item.description: Optional provider payload. Does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

item.name: 2e. Create Registry (Bedrock)
item.description: Optional provider payload. Does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

item.name: 2f. Create Registry (Azure OAuth Service Principal)
item.description: Default Azure auth mode: OAuth via service principal (auth.type=azure with tenant_id, client_id, client_secret). Optional provider payload; does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

item.name: 2g. Create Registry (Azure API Key)
item.description: Optional provider payload. Does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

item.name: 2h. Create Registry (Mistral)
item.description: Optional provider payload. Does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

item.name: 2i. Create Registry (Groq)
item.description: Optional provider payload. Does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

item.name: 2j. Create Registry (DeepSeek)
item.description: Optional provider payload. Does not overwrite registry_id used by later E2E steps.

exec: // The key is generated server-side and only surfaced here; the proxy
// step authenticates with it via the fixed X-AG-API-Key header.

request.description: Binds the OpenAI registry and its model policy atomically via the nested `registries[].model_policies` field. Uses `gpt-4o-mini` to match step 6.
Optional: registry was already bound in step 5a; this attach is idempotent (ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING).

header.description: Gateway discovery in header mode; in subdomain mode use Host {slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN> instead.

item.name: E2E Flow (OAuth2 JWT)
item.description: End-to-end inline consumer authenticated with classic OAuth2 (Bearer JWT validated via JWKS/introspection). Set `openai_api_key`, `oauth2_issuer`, `oauth2_jwks_url` and `oauth2_bearer_token` before running.

request.description: Classic OAuth: validates Bearer JWTs from an external authorization server. Inline consumers only.

header.description: OAuth2 access token or JWT accepted by the gateway oauth2 auth config.

item.name: E2E Role-Based Flow (OIDC)
item.description: End-to-end role-based provisioning: OpenAI registry, role with oidc_mapping and model_policies, OIDC auth credential, role_based consumer, then a proxy call authenticated with a Bearer JWT. Set `openai_api_key`, `idp_issuer`, `idp_jwks_url` and `idp_bearer_token` (JWT whose claims match the role oidc_mapping) before running.

request.description: model_policies cannot be set on create; bind registries first, then update the role.
model_policies may only reference registries already attached to the role.
Validates OIDC-issued JWTs; claims are matched against role oidc_mapping rules. Role-based consumers only.
Role-based consumers authorize through roles and authenticate via IDP. They cannot hold registries, lb_config or fallback.

header.description: OIDC-issued JWT whose claims satisfy the role oidc_mapping.
Gateway discovery in header mode; in subdomain mode use Host {slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN> instead.

item.name: E2E Role-Based Flow (Entra ID / OIDC)
item.description:
End-to-end role-based provisioning authenticated with Microsoft Entra ID. Same shape as 'E2E Role-Based Flow (OIDC)', but the auth is an `oidc` credential wired to Entra (issuer https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant>/v2.0 + the tenant JWKS). Role-based consumers REQUIRE an `oidc` auth: `oauth2` and `api_key` are rejected (Forbidden) for `role_based`. Roles are resolved by matching the Entra token claims against the role `oidc_mapping`.

Why `oidc` and not `oauth2`? `oauth2` is for inline consumers / the MCP brokered OAuth flow; it never resolves roles. For the role-based LLM proxy you need `oidc`, and the `client_secret` is NOT used (the gateway only validates the JWT via JWKS, it does not call Entra).

Set before running: `entra_tenant_id`, `entra_client_id`, `openai_api_key`, `entra_app_role` (an App Role value you exposed and assigned in Entra) and `entra_bearer_token` (an Entra-issued JWT carrying that role in its `roles` claim).

Entra prerequisites: App Registration manifest `requestedAccessTokenVersion: 2` (so tokens carry the /v2.0 issuer) and an App Role (appRoles[].value) assigned to the user/service principal so the access token includes a `roles` claim. If you prefer group claims, change the role `oidc_mapping` path to `groups` and use the group object IDs.

exec: console.log('token claims', { iss: claims.iss, aud: claims.aud, oid: claims.oid, roles: claims.roles });
pm.test('v2.0 issuer (else set requestedAccessTokenVersion=2 in the manifest)', () => pm.expect(claims.iss).to.include('/v2.0'));
pm.test('roles claim contains ' + wantRole + ' (assign the App Role in Entra)', () => pm.expect(claims.roles || []).to.include(wantRole));

request.description:
Machine-to-machine token (client_credentials) captured into {{entra_bearer_token}} for step 10. The scope MUST be your own app's `.default` (a Graph scope yields a token the gateway cannot validate). client-credentials tokens carry APPLICATION app roles in the `roles` claim (no `scp`): define an App Role with allowedMemberTypes ["Application"] and value = {{entra_app_role}}, add it as an application permission of this app and grant admin consent, otherwise `roles` is empty and the role oidc_mapping matches nothing.

For a real USER token instead (delegated, so the user's assigned app roles/groups appear), use the device-code grant: POST /devicecode with scope `api://{client_id}/mcp.access openid offline_access`, open the verification_uri, then POST /token with grant_type urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code.

oidc_mapping matches the Entra token claims. Here it matches the `roles` claim (Entra App Roles) against {{entra_app_role}}. To use group claims instead, set path to `groups` and values to the group object IDs (and enable group claims on the app). model_policies cannot be set on create; bind registries first, then update the role.
model_policies may only reference registries already attached to the role.

Role-based consumers MUST use an `oidc` auth; `oauth2`/`api_key` are rejected (Forbidden) for role_based. No `client_secret` here: the gateway only validates the Entra JWT via JWKS, it never calls Entra. `audiences` must equal the token `aud` (api://{client_id} or the bare client_id GUID - verify by decoding the token at jwt.ms). `subject_claim` `oid` is Entra's stable per-tenant user id (use `sub` if you prefer the pairwise per-app id).
Role-based consumers authorize through roles and authenticate via IDP. They cannot hold registries, lb_config or fallback.

header.description: Entra-issued JWT whose `roles` (or `groups`) claim satisfies the role oidc_mapping. Its `aud` must match the auth `audiences` and `iss` the configured issuer.
Gateway discovery in header mode; in subdomain mode use Host {slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN> instead.

item.name: E2E Role-Based Flow (Okta / IDP)
item.description:
End-to-end role-based provisioning authenticated with Okta. Same shape as 'E2E Role-Based Flow (Entra ID / OIDC)', but the `oidc` auth is wired to an Okta authorization server (issuer https://{okta_domain}/oauth2/{okta_auth_server_id} + that server's JWKS). Role-based consumers REQUIRE an `oidc` auth: `oauth2` and `api_key` are rejected (Forbidden) for `role_based`. Roles are resolved by matching the Okta token claims against the role `oidc_mapping`.

Set before running: `okta_domain` (e.g. dev-12345.okta.com, no scheme), `okta_auth_server_id` (e.g. `default`), `okta_client_id`, `okta_client_secret`, `okta_scope` (a scope granted to the app, e.g. `mcp.access`), `okta_audience` (the authorization server audience, e.g. `api://default`), `okta_claim` (claim path the role matches, default `scp` for the M2M client_credentials flow), `okta_group` (the value to match in that claim) and `openai_api_key`.

Where do roles come from? Okta has no Entra-style App Roles. For a user token (device-code / auth-code) map the `groups` claim (add a Groups claim on the authorization server). For a machine-to-machine token (client_credentials, used here) there is no user, so add a custom claim on the authorization server that emits the role/group value into the access token (set `okta_claim` to that claim name), or set `okta_claim` to `scp` and `okta_group` to a granted scope to match on scopes instead. The gateway only validates the JWT via JWKS; `okta_client_secret` is never used by the gateway.

exec: console.log('token claims', { iss: claims.iss, aud: claims.aud, sub: claims.sub, scp: claims.scp, claim: claims[claimPath] });
pm.test('claim ' + claimPath + ' contains ' + wantGroup + ' (add the claim on the Okta authorization server)', () => pm.expect(claims[claimPath] || []).to.include(wantGroup));

request.description:
Machine-to-machine token (client_credentials) captured into {{okta_bearer_token}} for step 10. The scope MUST be a custom scope defined on the authorization server and granted to this app (Okta rejects unknown scopes); openid/profile/email are user-only and won't work for client_credentials. The `aud` equals the authorization server audience ({{okta_audience}}, default api://default). There is no user, so `groups` is empty unless you add a custom claim on the authorization server that emits the role value into the access token (then set {{okta_claim}} to that claim name). Alternatively set {{okta_claim}}=scp and {{okta_group}} to a granted scope to match on scopes.

For a real USER token instead (so the user's Okta groups appear in `groups`), use the auth-code or device-code grant with scopes `openid profile groups {{okta_scope}}` and a Groups claim configured on the authorization server.

oidc_mapping matches the Okta token claims. By default it matches the `{{okta_claim}}` claim (scp, the granted scope) against {{okta_group}}. For an M2M token set {{okta_claim}} to the custom claim you added on the authorization server (or to `scp` to match a granted scope). model_policies cannot be set on create; bind registries first, then update the role.
model_policies may only reference registries already attached to the role.

Role-based consumers MUST use an `oidc` auth; `oauth2`/`api_key` are rejected (Forbidden) for role_based. No `client_secret` here: the gateway only validates the Okta JWT via JWKS, it never calls Okta. `issuer` and `jwks_url` come from the authorization server's /.well-known/openid-configuration. `audiences` must equal the token `aud` (the authorization server audience, default api://default; verify by decoding the token). `subject_claim` `sub` is the Okta subject (the client_id for M2M, the user id for user tokens).
Role-based consumers authorize through roles and authenticate via IDP. They cannot hold registries, lb_config or fallback.

header.description: Okta-issued JWT whose `{{okta_claim}}` claim satisfies the role oidc_mapping. Its `aud` must match the auth `audiences` and `iss` the configured issuer.
Gateway discovery in header mode; in subdomain mode use Host {slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN> instead.

item.name: MCP Gateway
item.description:
One folder per flow, every folder SELF-CONTAINED: it creates its own gateway, auths, registries and consumers, exercises the flow, and tears everything down at the end. Run any single folder top to bottom - no cross-folder prerequisites.

Folders:
1. Flow A - Simple proxy: one upstream (DeepWiki) behind a virtual MCP, API-key auth, JSON-RPC surface, toolkit curation.
2. Flow B - Composition hub: several upstream MCP servers federated behind one virtual path.
3. Flow C - Inbound OAuth with Microsoft Entra ID (JWT validation, M2M tokens, anti-downgrade).
4. Flow D - OAuth 2.1 discovery surface: every step an MCP client (Cursor) performs automatically.
5. Flow E - Downstream credentials: registry auth modes (static, exchange/STS, forwarded manual/auto).
6. Flow F - Linear end-to-end: forwarded + auto registration (DCR), consent flow, Cursor walkthrough.
7. Flow G - Full MCP surface: prompts, resources and resource templates federation, toolkit as per-surface allowlist (needs the local 'everything' reference server).
8. Diagnostics - admin introspection, catalog, health (standalone utilities).

Folders share collection variable slots (gateway_id, registry_id, consumer_id, ...): run ONE folder at a time, top to bottom, finishing with its Cleanup. Jumping between half-run folders overwrites the ids.

IMPORTANT (Entra folders C, D, F): the admin API rejects (409) a second ENABLED oauth2 auth covering the same (issuer, audience) pair. Run a folder's Cleanup before starting another Entra folder, or the 'Create oauth2 auth' step fails with 409.

Collection variables to set first:
- admin_url (default http://localhost:8080)
- mcp_url (default http://localhost:8082)
- admin_token (mint with: set -a; source .env; set +a; bash scripts/generate_jwt_token.sh)

For flows C, D and F also set entra_tenant_id / entra_client_id / entra_client_secret. Entra app prerequisites (skipping any causes a silent 'Authenticating...' loop in Cursor):
1. Expose an API: Application ID URI api://{client_id} and a SCOPE named mcp.access (admins and users) - else AADSTS65005.
2. API permissions: add the app's own delegated mcp.access permission, grant admin consent.
3. Manifest: requestedAccessTokenVersion: 2 (tokens must carry the /v2.0 issuer).
4. Authentication: Web redirect URI {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback.

MULTI-TENANT (enterprise): multiple gateways/consumers may share the same IdP. Resolution is path-first: the request path picks the consumer(s) and only their ATTACHED auths can authenticate the request. The AS facade is resource-scoped: when several issuers are enabled, clients must send the RFC 8707 'resource' parameter (the virtual MCP URL) on authorize and token requests so the gateway picks the right IdP; with a single issuer the resource is optional. Guardrails: the admin API rejects (409) a second ENABLED oauth2 auth covering the same (issuer, audience) pair - scope each tenant with a distinct audience. Optional host isolation: set 'domain' on a gateway (e.g. tenant-a.gw.example.com) and requests carrying that Host resolve against that gateway only.

item.name: 1. Flow A - Simple proxy (API key + DeepWiki)
item.description:
Self-contained. One upstream MCP server behind one virtual path with API-key auth: gateway -> auth -> registry -> consumer -> attach -> JSON-RPC surface -> toolkit curation -> cleanup. DeepWiki needs no upstream auth, so this flow works out of the box.

MCP endpoint: {{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp - the consumer slug is generated on creation and captured by the create request.

request.description: Optionally add "domain": "tenant-a.gw.example.com" (bare hostname) for host-based tenant isolation: requests carrying that Host header resolve against this gateway only. Domains are unique across gateways. Ownership tenant_id is required (JWT claim or body for platform admins).
The api_key value is only returned once, on create; the test script stores it in {{api_key}}.
The consumer now exposes exactly one tool, renamed. Re-run tools/list to verify.
Resources ARE federated now (see Flow G), but DeepWiki is a tools-only upstream, so the merged list is empty here.
Prompts ARE federated now (see Flow G), but DeepWiki is a tools-only upstream, so the merged list is empty here.
Children first, gateway last (else 409).

item.name: 2. Flow B - Composition hub (many servers, one MCP)
item.description:
Self-contained. Federate several upstream MCP servers behind ONE consumer: builds it from DeepWiki and Hugging Face, proves federation through a single tools/list and per-upstream routing, then curates a cross-server toolkit, then cleans up.

Naming: tools keep their upstream name unless two registries expose the same name, in which case the composer auto-prefixes them with the registry name ({registry}_{tool}). The toolkit shows the curated alternative: cherry-pick and rename explicitly.

item.name: Create MCP registry (Hugging Face, auth none)

request.description: Second upstream. Hugging Face's MCP works anonymously (rate-limited); both servers are listed in GET /v1/mcp-servers-catalog.
fail_mode=open: if one upstream is unreachable, the hub still serves the tools of the healthy ones (closed would fail the whole consumer).

item.name: Attach Hugging Face registry to hub

request.description: One list, two servers: DeepWiki's ask_question / read_wiki_* next to Hugging Face's hub_repo_search / paper_search / hf_doc_*. No name collisions here, so no prefixes are added.
The composer resolves the tool to the DeepWiki registry and proxies the call there with that registry's credentials (none here).
Same virtual path, different upstream: routed to Hugging Face.

item.name: Curate hub toolkit (cherry-pick + rename across servers)

request.description: The hub now exposes exactly three tools: wiki_ask (DeepWiki), hf_models (renamed) and paper_search (original name) from Hugging Face. Everything else is hidden. Re-run tools/list to verify; PUT {"toolkit": []} to expose everything again.
Clients only ever see the curated name; the composer maps hf_models back to hub_repo_search on the Hugging Face upstream.

item.name: 3. Flow C - Inbound OAuth (Entra ID)
item.description:
Self-contained. JWT validation with Microsoft Entra ID: gateway -> oauth2 auth -> DeepWiki registry -> consumer -> M2M token -> authenticated JSON-RPC -> anti-downgrade -> cleanup. See the collection description for the Entra app prerequisites.

MCP endpoint: {{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp - the consumer slug is generated on creation and captured by the create request.

NOTE: only one ENABLED oauth2 auth may cover the same (issuer, audience) pair - run another Entra folder's Cleanup first or 'Create oauth2 auth' answers 409.

request.description: issuer must match the token's iss claim exactly (v2.0 suffix); jwks_url is resolved via OIDC discovery. client_id + client_secret are what the AS facade uses to broker the browser authorization-code flow against Entra (redirect URI {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback must be registered as a Web platform redirect on the Entra app). required_scopes must be FULLY QUALIFIED (api://{client_id}/mcp.access): the facade forwards them verbatim to Entra's authorize endpoint, which rejects bare leaf names; the scope must actually EXIST under the app's 'Expose an API' or Entra fails with AADSTS65005. audiences accepts either form: Entra v2.0 tokens carry the bare client id GUID in aud while v1.0 used the api:// URI - the validator treats them as equivalent. offline_access makes Entra return a refresh token; it is request-only and never enforced against access tokens (protocol scopes - openid/profile/email/offline_access - are skipped by inbound scope validation). 409 here means another ENABLED oauth2 auth already covers this (issuer, audience): run that folder's Cleanup or disable it.
Used only by the 'Invalid JWT is rejected' request to prove the chain never falls back from a failed Bearer to a valid API key.
Consumer-first resolution: only auths ATTACHED to this consumer can authenticate requests to its MCP endpoint ({{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp).

exec: console.log('token claims', { iss: claims.iss, aud: claims.aud, oid: claims.oid, roles: claims.roles });
pm.test('v2.0 issuer (else set requestedAccessTokenVersion=2 in the manifest)', () => {

request.description: M2M token for Postman testing; the scope must be your own app's .default (a Graph scope produces a token TrustGate cannot validate). NOTE: client-credentials tokens carry app permissions in the roles claim (no scp). For required_scopes validation to pass, the Entra app must also define an APPLICATION permission (app role) named mcp.access and assign it to itself - otherwise this M2M token is rejected with 401 and only the browser user flow works.
Path-first: the request path resolves the consumer, then the token is validated against the consumer's ATTACHED oauth2 auth (signature via JWKS/OIDC discovery, issuer, audience, scopes).

exec: pm.test('401 with challenge, no API-key fallback', () => {

request.description: Sends a bogus Bearer token AND a valid API key: the chain takes the Bearer path first and fails without falling back to the API key (anti-downgrade by design).
Frees the (issuer, audience) pair so flows D and F can create their own Entra auth without a 409.

item.name: 4. Flow D - OAuth 2.1 discovery (what MCP clients do)
item.description:
Self-contained. Sets up a protected MCP consumer (addressed by its generated slug, {{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp) with an Entra oauth2 auth, then replays every step an MCP client (Cursor) performs automatically: 401 challenge -> protected-resource metadata (RFC 9728) -> AS metadata (RFC 8414) -> DCR (RFC 7591) -> brokered authorize -> cleanup.

NOTE: only one ENABLED oauth2 auth may cover the same (issuer, audience) pair - run another Entra folder's Cleanup first or 'Create oauth2 auth' answers 409.

request.description: See Flow C's 'Create oauth2 auth' for the full field-by-field explanation. 409 here means another ENABLED oauth2 auth already covers this (issuer, audience): run that folder's Cleanup or disable it.
No credential on purpose: the MCP plane answers 401 + WWW-Authenticate: Bearer resource_metadata="..." so MCP clients bootstrap the OAuth flow.

item.name: GET on the MCP path answers 405 (SSE not offered)

exec: pm.test('405 Method Not Allowed with Allow: POST', () => {

request.description: Streamable HTTP clients probe GET for the optional server-initiated SSE stream. The gateway does not offer one and must answer 405 per spec - a 401/404 here makes clients misread it as a credential problem and loop on re-authentication. Same for DELETE (session termination).
Lists the authorization servers (issuers of enabled oauth2 auths) that can issue tokens for this MCP plane.
Same document scoped to a specific virtual MCP path, per the MCP authorization spec: scopes_supported reflects only the auths attached to the consumer on this path.

The gateway advertises itself as the authorization server (AS facade): authorize/token/register endpoints all point at the gateway, which brokers to the IdP. 404 until an oauth2 auth exists. With several distinct issuers enabled, clients must pass the RFC 8707 resource parameter on authorize/token so the facade picks the right IdP.
Returns the registered public client (PKCE). 400 if no oauth2 auth has a client_id configured.

exec: pm.test('302 to Entra with scope and PKCE', () => {

request.description: Replays the first leg of the browser flow without a browser. The Location header must point at login.microsoftonline.com AND carry a scope parameter (api://{client_id}/mcp.access). The resource parameter (RFC 8707, the virtual MCP URL) selects which consumer's IdP brokers the flow - required when several issuers are enabled on the plane, optional with one. The same parameter is honored on POST /oauth/token (refresh grants). A redirect without scope means the facade picked a stale oauth2 auth entry without required_scopes - find and delete it.
Verification keys for TrustGate-minted downstream tokens (Flow E exchange modes). Configure upstreams to trust iss = STS_ISSUER with this JWKS.

item.name: 5. Flow E - Downstream credentials (STS & forwarded)
item.description: Self-contained. How the gateway authenticates AGAINST upstream MCP servers, per registry (mcp_target.auth.mode): none, static (fixed header), passthrough, exchange (STS-minted or IdP-brokered tokens), forwarded (per-user third-party OAuth vaulted by the gateway). Creates a scratch gateway + registry, applies each auth mode in turn as examples, then cleans up - adapt url/audience to a real upstream.

request.description: Scratch registry the following PUT requests mutate to demonstrate each downstream auth mode.
Service-account style: one fixed credential for all principals (e.g. Stripe or Context7 API keys). The value is write-only: reads return it masked.

The STS mints a short-lived RS256 JWT (same sub, aud = audience, iss = TrustGate) per principal and injects it as Authorization on every upstream connection. Use pattern=delegation (+actor), obo (+scope) or token_exchange (+audience) for the other RFC 8693 patterns.

item.name: Set registry auth: forwarded + manual registration (GitHub)

request.description: Manual registration: the gateway uses an admin pre-registered OAuth app. Set the OAuth app callback to {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback/github. After this, a tools call by a user without a linked account returns JSON-RPC error -32003 with data.connect_url - Flow F shows the full consent loop.

item.name: Set registry auth: forwarded + auto registration (DCR)

request.description: Auto registration: no OAuth app, no client secret. At consent time the gateway discovers the upstream's authorization server via its MCP protected-resource metadata (RFC 9728/8414) and registers itself once via Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591) as a public client with PKCE. Works with any spec-compliant remote MCP (Linear, Notion, ...); scopes and the RFC 8707 resource indicator default from the upstream's metadata. If the upstream does not publish OAuth metadata, the connect flow returns an error telling you to fall back to manual registration. Flow F runs this for real against Linear.

item.name: 6. Flow F - Linear end-to-end (forwarded + auto, Cursor)
item.description:
Self-contained. The full story on real services: inbound Entra ID, downstream Linear with forwarded mode + auto registration - no Linear OAuth app or client secret needed.

Run the admin requests top to bottom, then 'Get Entra token', then the MCP plane requests. The first tools/list returns -32003 with data.connect_url: open it in a browser, click Connect Linear, approve, re-run. Finish with the Cleanup requests.

MCP endpoint: {{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp - the consumer slug is generated on creation and captured by the create request.

In Cursor you skip the Postman token entirely: add {{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp to mcp.json and Cursor bootstraps the OAuth flow from the 401 challenge (Flow D shows every step it performs). After the Entra login, if you have unlinked providers the browser detours to the connect page (chained consent) - link Linear, click Continue, done. Already linked? The browser hands the code straight back to Cursor via its cursor:// deep link ('Open Cursor?' dialog; tick 'Always allow' to never see it again).

Note (M2M): the Postman client-credentials token links Linear to the service principal. Real users in Cursor each link their own Linear account.

NOTE: only one ENABLED oauth2 auth may cover the same (issuer, audience) pair - run another Entra folder's Cleanup first or 'Create oauth2 auth' answers 409.

request.description: See Flow C's 'Create oauth2 auth' for the full field-by-field explanation. 409 here means another ENABLED oauth2 auth already covers this (issuer, audience): run that folder's Cleanup or disable it.

registration: auto means zero per-provider config: at consent time the gateway fetches https://mcp.linear.app/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/mcp, resolves Linear's authorization server (RFC 8414), registers itself once via DCR (RFC 7591) as a public client with PKCE, and stores the issued client_id in Redis keyed (gateway, registry). Scopes and the RFC 8707 resource indicator default from Linear's metadata.
Wildcard exposes every Linear tool. Replace with explicit {tool, expose_as} entries to curate or rename.

exec: console.log('token claims', { iss: claims.iss, aud: claims.aud, oid: claims.oid, roles: claims.roles });
pm.test('v2.0 issuer (else set requestedAccessTokenVersion=2 in the manifest)', () => {

request.description: M2M token for Postman testing; the scope must be your own app's .default (a Graph scope produces a token TrustGate cannot validate). NOTE: client-credentials tokens carry app permissions in the roles claim (no scp). For required_scopes validation to pass, the Entra app must also define an APPLICATION permission (app role) named mcp.access and assign it to itself - otherwise this M2M token is rejected with 401 and only the browser user flow works.
Get {{entra_token}} first via this folder's 'Get Entra token (client credentials)'.

item.name: tools/list -> consent error on first run

exec: console.log('Open in a browser to link Linear:', url);

request.description: First run: no vaulted Linear token for this principal, so the gateway answers JSON-RPC error -32003 with data.connect_url (the script saves the ticket and logs the URL). Open it in a browser, click Connect Linear - the gateway performs upstream discovery + DCR right there - approve at Linear, then re-run: the result lists Linear's tools.

The ticket-authenticated consent page: one button per forwarded provider behind this virtual MCP, with linked/unlinked status. Normally opened in a browser via the connect_url from the -32003 error, or automatically right after the Entra leg (chained consent). Tickets expire after 15 minutes.

Check the exact tool names in the tools/list result (the composer prefixes with the registry name only on collisions). The call runs with YOUR vaulted Linear token; another principal calling the same consumer gets their own consent flow.
Deletes the vaulted credential for (gateway, principal, linear). The next tools call returns the -32003 consent error again - also handy to re-trigger the chained consent page.

item.name: 7. Flow G - Full MCP surface (prompts, resources, toolkit governance)
item.description:
Self-contained. Demonstrates the full federated MCP surface - tools, prompts, resources, resource templates - and the toolkit as a per-surface allowlist.

PREREQUISITE - start the reference 'everything' MCP server locally (it exposes tools, prompts AND resources):

    npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-everything streamableHttp

It listens on http://localhost:3001/mcp. The registry below uses host.docker.internal:3001 so the dockerized gateway can reach it; change it to localhost:3001 if you run the gateway natively.

Flow: full surface first (everything exposed - empty toolkit), then curate the toolkit and watch each surface shrink to the allowlist: one tool, one renamed prompt, one resource URI. Reads outside the allowlist fail with the spec error -32002.

Toolkit semantics: an EMPTY toolkit exposes every tool, prompt and resource. A NON-EMPTY toolkit is an allowlist per surface - a registry with only tool entries exposes NO prompts and NO resources. Resource selectors accept exact URIs, trailing-asterisk prefixes (repo://github/*) or *.

MCP endpoint: {{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp - the consumer slug is generated on creation and captured by the create request.

request.description: The api_key value is only returned once, on create; the test script stores it in {{api_key}}.

The reference server @modelcontextprotocol/server-everything: tools (echo, add, ...), prompts (simple_prompt, complex_prompt) and 100 test resources (test://static/resource/{1..100}) plus a resource template.
The gateway negotiates the protocol version (echoes 2025-06-18) and advertises tools, resources AND prompts capabilities.
Empty toolkit: every upstream tool is exposed.
Empty toolkit: every upstream prompt is exposed. Name collisions across registries would be auto-prefixed {server}_{prompt}, like tools.
Routed to the owning upstream and rendered there; the result passes through verbatim.
Resources are URI-addressed: merged across upstreams, no renaming.
The gateway routes the URI to the upstream that lists it; template-addressed URIs fall back to trying each resource-capable upstream.

One entry per surface: only the echo tool, only simple_prompt (renamed demo_prompt), only resource URI test://static/resource/1. Everything else disappears from the virtual MCP. Exactly one of tool|prompt|resource per entry; expose_as renames tools and prompts (not resources).
The toolkit is now a per-surface allowlist: expect exactly one tool.
simple_prompt is exposed under its alias demo_prompt; complex_prompt is gone.
The alias resolves back to the upstream prompt name. Calling the original name simple_prompt now fails.
Only URIs matching a resource entry survive. Use a prefix selector (test://static/resource/*) to allow a subtree.
The URI is outside the allowlist: expect JSON-RPC error -32002 (resource not found). Enforced at read time too, so template-expanded URIs cannot bypass the list filter.

item.name: 8. Flow H - Inbound OAuth + discovery (Okta)
item.description:
Self-contained. Interactive MCP login brokered through Okta as the identity provider: gateway -> oauth2 auth (Okta) -> DeepWiki registry -> MCP consumer -> the OAuth 2.1 discovery surface an MCP client (Cursor) replays automatically -> M2M-authenticated JSON-RPC -> anti-downgrade -> cleanup.

Consumers have NO path field: the server assigns a random slug on create and the virtual MCP endpoint is {{mcp_url}}/{slug}/mcp. The 'Create MCP consumer' step captures it into {{consumer_slug}}.

Why `oauth2` and not `oidc`? For MCP, the gateway BROKERS the browser authorization-code flow (the client has no Okta token yet): it needs client_id + client_secret to drive Okta, which only the `oauth2` auth type carries. An `oidc` auth only VALIDATES a token the caller already has (LLM proxy), so an MCP consumer is rejected if you attach an `oidc` auth. 'Okta as IDP' here means Okta is the identity provider behind an `oauth2` auth.

Role-based variant: an MCP consumer may also be role_based; it still requires exactly one `oauth2` auth, plus a role whose oidc_mapping matches the Okta token claims (see 'E2E Role-Based Flow (Okta / IDP)' for the role + oidc_mapping shape). This folder uses an inline MCP consumer to keep the OAuth surface front and center.

Set before running: `okta_domain` (e.g. dev-12345.okta.com, no scheme), `okta_auth_server_id` (e.g. `default`), `okta_client_id`, `okta_client_secret`, `okta_scope` (a custom scope exposed AND granted on the authorization server, e.g. `mcp.access`) and `okta_audience` (the authorization server audience, e.g. `api://default`).

Okta app prerequisites (skipping any makes Cursor loop on 'Authenticating...'): 1) an Authorization Server (Security > API) with a custom scope = {{okta_scope}} and audience = {{okta_audience}}; 2) an OIDC app with the Authorization Code grant and a Web redirect URI {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback; 3) for the M2M test step below, the Client Credentials grant enabled and the scope granted to the app. The gateway validates the Okta JWT via JWKS; the client_secret is only used to broker the browser flow.

NOTE: only one ENABLED oauth2 auth may cover the same (issuer, audience) pair on the plane - run another oauth2 folder's Cleanup first or 'Create oauth2 auth (Okta)' answers 409.

request.description: issuer must equal the token's iss claim exactly (the authorization server base URL, https://{domain}/oauth2/{authServerId}). jwks_url is the server's signing keys ({issuer}/v1/keys); it can be omitted to let the gateway resolve it via OIDC discovery. client_id + client_secret let the AS facade broker the browser authorization-code flow against Okta (redirect URI {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback must be a Web redirect on the Okta app). required_scopes must be a custom scope EXPOSED on the authorization server (Okta rejects unknown scopes; openid/profile/email are user-only). audiences must match the token aud (the authorization server audience, default api://default). 409 means another ENABLED oauth2 auth already covers this (issuer, audience): run that folder's Cleanup or scope this one with a distinct audience.
Used only by 'Invalid JWT is rejected' to prove the chain never falls back from a failed Bearer to a valid API key.

Consumers have no path: the server assigns a random slug and the MCP endpoint is {{mcp_url}}/{slug}/mcp. The test captures the slug into {{consumer_slug}}; every MCP call below addresses {{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp.

For the role-based variant add "routing_mode": "role_based" here, then create a role with an oidc_mapping over the Okta claims and attach it - the oauth2 auth stays the same.
Path-first resolution: only auths ATTACHED to the consumer can authenticate or broker requests to {{mcp_url}}/{{consumer_slug}}/mcp.
No credential on purpose: the MCP plane answers 401 + WWW-Authenticate: Bearer resource_metadata="..." so MCP clients bootstrap the OAuth flow.

Document scoped to this virtual MCP path ({{consumer_slug}}/mcp): authorization_servers lists the Okta issuer of the oauth2 auth attached to the consumer, and scopes_supported reflects only that auth.

The gateway advertises itself as the authorization server (AS facade): authorize/token/register point at the gateway, which brokers to Okta. With several distinct issuers enabled, clients must pass the RFC 8707 resource parameter (the virtual MCP URL) on authorize/token so the facade picks the right IdP.
Returns the registered public client (PKCE). 400 if the resolved oauth2 auth has no client_id configured.

exec: pm.test('302 to Okta with scope and PKCE', () => {

request.description: Replays the first leg of the browser flow without a browser. The Location header must point at the Okta authorization server AND carry a scope parameter ({{okta_scope}}) and the PKCE code_challenge. The resource parameter (RFC 8707, the virtual MCP URL) selects which consumer's IdP brokers the flow - required when several issuers are enabled, optional with one.

exec: console.log('token claims', { iss: claims.iss, aud: claims.aud, sub: claims.sub, scp: claims.scp });
pm.test('issuer matches the Okta authorization server', () => pm.expect(claims.iss).to.include('/oauth2/'));

request.description: M2M token for Postman testing, captured into {{okta_bearer_token}}. The scope MUST be a custom scope defined on the authorization server and granted to this app; openid/profile/email are user-only and won't work for client_credentials. The aud equals the authorization server audience ({{okta_audience}}). For a real USER token (browser/device-code) the same token validates - this step only avoids the interactive browser leg in Postman.
Path-first: the request path resolves the consumer, then the token is validated against the consumer's ATTACHED oauth2 auth (signature via JWKS, issuer, audience, required_scopes).

exec: pm.test('401 with challenge, no API-key fallback', () => {

request.description: Sends a bogus Bearer token AND a valid API key: the chain takes the Bearer path first and fails without falling back to the API key (anti-downgrade by design).
Frees the (issuer, audience) pair so other oauth2 folders can create their own Okta auth without a 409. Detach order: consumer first (else the auth is still referenced).
Children first, gateway last (else 409).

item.name: 9. Flow I - GitHub MCP (oauth2 inbound + forwarded upstream, NO role)
item.description:
Minimal provisioning, no roles. Four admin steps as requested:
1. Create registry -> GitHub (MCP, forwarded upstream GitHub OAuth).
2. Create auth type oauth2 -> GitHub (inbound, session_mode for GitHub's opaque tokens).
3. Create consumer (type MCP).
4. Associate auth + registry to the consumer.
(A gateway is created first as the parent container, and Cleanup deletes everything at the end.)

Prereqs: a GitHub OAuth app. Set github_client_id / github_client_secret. Authorization callback URL = {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback (inbound) and {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback/github (upstream) - register both.

HEADS-UP about the inbound oauth2 -> GitHub: creation of all four resources succeeds (the admin API accepts a session_mode GitHub auth). But the runtime interactive login at GET /oauth/authorize resolves the IdP's authorize/token endpoints via issuer discovery ({issuer}/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server then /openid-configuration). github.com returns 404 on both (verified), so the browser login leg fails with 'lacks authorization/token endpoints' (HTTP 500) until the gateway gains a way to set authorize_url/token_url for inbound (today only the upstream/forwarded leg has those). M2M (Bearer-token) inbound is unaffected. The four resources below are still created exactly as asked.

request.description: UPSTREAM GitHub OAuth. forwarded + manual: the gateway drives a per-user authorization-code flow against your GitHub OAuth app (github.com publishes no discovery, hence explicit authorize_url/token_url). GitHub app Authorization callback URL must be {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback/github. client_secret is write-only (reads return it masked).

item.name: 2. Create auth type oauth2 -> GitHub (inbound, session_mode)

request.description: INBOUND oauth2 -> GitHub. GitHub publishes no authorization-server discovery, so authorize_url/token_url are pinned explicitly (the gateway skips the {issuer}/.well-known lookup when both are set). session_mode mints gateway session tokens from GitHub's opaque access tokens; subject_claim=id reads the stable user id from userinfo_url. GitHub OAuth app Authorization callback URL must be {{mcp_url}}/oauth/callback. client_id is required (pre-registered client). 409 means another ENABLED oauth2 auth already covers this (issuer, audience). For a discovery-capable IdP (Okta/Entra/Auth0/Google) omit authorize_url/token_url/session_mode and just set issuer + jwks_url.

item.name: 10. Diagnostics (standalone)
item.description: Utilities that need no setup of their own. The id-based requests (introspect/get) read whatever {{gateway_id}}/{{registry_id}}/{{consumer_id}} the last-run flow stored - run them mid-flow, before that flow's Cleanup.

exec: console.log('Multiple gateways may share an IdP, but no two ENABLED oauth2 auths may cover the same (issuer, audience) pair - the admin API rejects that with 409.');
console.log('If an old gateway still has an enabled oauth2 auth with the same Entra issuer AND audience, delete it (or scope it with a different audience) before running an Entra flow.');

request.description: Lists gateways so you can spot leftovers from earlier flows that skipped their Cleanup. Stale enabled oauth2 auth entries sharing the Entra (issuer, audience) block the Entra folders with 409.

Lists the upstream's tools using the registry's configured credential. Works for auth modes none/static; forwarded-mode registries have no service-level credential, so introspection is empty until a principal links an account.
Curated list of well-known remote MCP servers with auth hints (none/static/oauth).

item.name: E2E Plugin Flows
item.description: End-to-end flows that exercise individual built-in plugins through the proxy plane. Each subfolder provisions its own gateway, registry, policy, API key and consumer, then drives requests that demonstrate the plugin's effect. Set `openai_api_key` to a real key before running and execute the requests top-to-bottom.

item.name: Model allowlist
item.description: Exercises the `model_allowlist` plugin at `pre_request`. The consumer model policy is intentionally permissive (it admits every model used below) so the plugin is the sole gate. Step 8 sends an allowed model (200). Step 9 sends a disallowed model and asserts the exact 403 `model_not_allowed` body. Steps 10-11 flip the policy to `substitute` and prove a disallowed model is transparently rewritten to an allowed one (200). The policy is single-plugin and sequential, so the in-place body rewrite is preserved.

request.description: The policy `slug` must equal the plugin name `model_allowlist`. `allowed_models` accepts `*` glob patterns; `gpt-4o*` matches `gpt-4o`, `gpt-4o-2024-...`, etc. `default_model` is injected when a request omits the model.
The consumer model policy admits every model used in this flow (including the one the plugin rejects) so that the `model_allowlist` plugin is the only thing gating the request.
`gpt-4o-mini` is in the plugin allowlist, so the request passes through to OpenAI unchanged.

exec: pm.test('error type is model_not_allowed', () => body.error && body.error.type === 'model_not_allowed');

request.description: `gpt-3.5-turbo` is admitted by the consumer model policy but is not in the plugin allowlist, so the plugin short-circuits with `403` and the exact body `{"error":{"type":"model_not_allowed","model":"gpt-3.5-turbo","allowed":[...]}}`; upstream is never contacted.
Switches the same policy to `substitute`: disallowed models are rewritten in place to `substitute_with` (which must itself match the allowlist) instead of being rejected.
Same disallowed `gpt-3.5-turbo` request as step 9, but the policy is now in `substitute` mode, so the plugin rewrites the body model to `gpt-4o-mini` and the request succeeds against OpenAI.

item.name: Prompt template
item.description: Exercises the `prompt_template` plugin at `pre_request`. Steps 1-7 build a gateway/registry/consumer with the plugin policy attached (policy `slug` must equal the plugin name `prompt_template`). Steps 8-9 cover Mode A (context injection): a templated system prompt is rendered from request context and injected; an unresolved `{{variable}}` with `on_missing_context_variable: error` returns `500 template_variable_unresolved`. Step 10 switches the policy to Mode B (named template reference) with `allow_untemplated_requests: false`; steps 11-15 prove rendering (`{template://support-bot@published}` + a top-level `properties` map replaces the messages array and `properties` never reaches the upstream) and the client-facing error codes. Plugin rejections share the shape `{"error":"plugin_rejected","type":<code>}`. The policy is single-plugin and sequential so the in-place body rewrite is preserved. NOTE: the `{{tenant_tier}}`, `{{persona}}` and `{{question}}` tokens are the plugin's own template placeholders; they are intentionally NOT Postman variables, so Postman forwards them literally.

request.description: Mode A: `context_variables.tenant_tier` is read from the `X-Tenant-Tier` request header and substituted into the `inject_templates` content, which is merged into the system prompt. `on_missing_context_variable: error` makes an unresolved variable a hard `500`. `allow_untemplated_requests: true` lets non-referencing requests pass.
The consumer model policy admits the model used below so the `prompt_template` plugin is the only thing transforming the request.

item.name: 8. Proxy - Mode A header present -> 200 (system injected)

request.description: `X-Tenant-Tier: enterprise` resolves `{{tenant_tier}}`, so the plugin injects/merges the system prompt 'You are a helpful assistant for enterprise customers.' and forwards the rewritten body to OpenAI.

item.name: 9. Proxy - Mode A header missing -> 500 template_variable_unresolved

request.description: Without `X-Tenant-Tier` the `{{tenant_tier}}` variable is unresolved; with `on_missing_context_variable: error` the plugin short-circuits with `500` and `{"error":"plugin_rejected","type":"template_variable_unresolved"}`; upstream is never contacted.

item.name: 10. Update Policy -> Mode B (named template reference)

request.description: Switches the same policy to Mode B: the client references `{template://support-bot@published}` and supplies a `properties` map. The version is resolved by label (`default_label: published`), `properties` are validated against `required_variables`, the rendered JSON messages replace the request messages array, and `properties` is stripped before forwarding. `allow_untemplated_requests: false` rejects requests with no reference.

item.name: 11. Proxy - Mode B render -> 200 (messages replaced, properties stripped)

request.description: The reference resolves to `support-bot` v3; `properties` (persona, question) render the template and replace the messages array with the rendered system+user pair. The literal `{template://...}` token and the `properties` field never reach OpenAI.

item.name: 12. Proxy - Mode B missing required var -> 400 template_variable_missing

request.description: `persona` is required but absent from `properties`, so the plugin rejects with `400 template_variable_missing` before contacting the upstream.

item.name: 13. Proxy - Mode B invalid enum -> 400 template_variable_invalid

request.description: `persona: rude` is not in the `enum` [friendly, formal], so the plugin rejects with `400 template_variable_invalid`.
No `named_templates` entry matches `does-not-exist`, so the plugin rejects with `400 template_not_found`.
With `allow_untemplated_requests: false` and no `{template://...}` reference in the request, the plugin rejects with `400 template_required`.

item.name: Tool allowlist
item.description: Exercises the `tool_allowlist` plugin at `pre_request`. The plugin glob-filters the request `tools[]` array before the model sees it. The first policy allow-lists `search_*`/`calculate` and rejects when the filter empties the array: an allowed request (200) and a request whose only tool is disallowed -> 403 `no_tools_allowed`. The final steps flip `on_empty_after_filter` to `strip_tools_field` and prove the same all-disallowed request now passes (200) with the tools removed from the forwarded body. Single-plugin sequential policy, so the body rewrite is preserved. Set `openai_api_key` to a real key before running.

request.description: The policy `slug` must equal the plugin name `tool_allowlist`. `allow_tools` accepts `*`/`?`/`[...]` globs matched against the tool name (`search_*` matches `search_web`, `search_docs`, etc.). `deny_tools` (not used here) is applied after the allow-list and overrides it. `on_empty_after_filter` controls behaviour when every tool is filtered out: `reject` (used here) short-circuits with `403 no_tools_allowed`, while `pass_through_empty`/`strip_tools_field` forward the request with the tools removed.
The consumer model policy admits the model used in this flow so that the `tool_allowlist` plugin is the only thing gating the request; the plugin operates on the `tools[]` array, not the model.
`search_web` matches the `search_*` glob and `calculate` is allow-listed verbatim, so the filter keeps both tools and the request passes through to OpenAI with its `tools[]` array intact.

exec: pm.test('error type is no_tools_allowed', () => body.error && body.error.type === 'no_tools_allowed');

request.description: `delete_db` matches neither `search_*` nor `calculate`, so the filter empties the `tools[]` array. With `on_empty_after_filter: reject` the plugin short-circuits at `pre_request` with `403` and the exact body `{"error":{"type":"no_tools_allowed","requested":["delete_db"],"allowed_after_filter":[]}}`; upstream is never contacted.

Switches the same policy to `strip_tools_field`: when the filter empties the `tools[]` array the plugin drops the `tools`/`tool_choice` fields from the forwarded body and lets the request through instead of rejecting it.

item.name: 11. Proxy - all tools disallowed, tools stripped -> 200

request.description: Same all-disallowed `delete_db` request as step 9, but the policy is now in `strip_tools_field` mode, so the plugin removes the `tools`/`tool_choice` fields and forwards the request to OpenAI, which answers normally with `200`.

item.name: Budget (token, dollar & cost cap)
item.description: Exercises the `token_rate_limiter` budget plugin through the proxy. The consumer model policy is permissive so the plugin is the sole gate. Steps 8-9 prove the stateless cost cap allows a cheap model and rejects an over-priced one with `403 model_too_expensive`. Steps 10-11 flip the cost cap to `downgrade` and prove an over-ceiling model is transparently rewritten to a same-provider cheaper model (`X-NeuralTrust-Model-Downgraded`). Steps 12-14 replace the cost cap with a cumulative token budget and prove the first request passes while the next is blocked with `429 token_budget_exceeded` and `X-Budget-*` headers. Pricing is `custom` for determinism; set `openai_api_key` to a real key and run top-to-bottom.

request.description: The policy `slug` must equal the plugin name `token_rate_limiter`. The stateless `cost_cap` runs at `pre_request`: it resolves each model's per-1k price from `custom_pricing` and rejects any model whose input or output price exceeds the ceiling. Here `gpt-4o` (input $1/1k, output $2/1k) is over the $0.5 ceiling while `gpt-4o-mini` (input $0.1/1k, output $0.1/1k) is under it.
The consumer model policy admits both `gpt-4o` and `gpt-4o-mini` so that the `token_rate_limiter` plugin (cost cap / budget) is the only gate exercised by this flow.
`gpt-4o-mini` is priced below the cost cap ceiling, so the cost cap allows it and the request reaches OpenAI unchanged.

exec: pm.test('error type is model_too_expensive', () => body.error && body.error.type === 'model_too_expensive');
pm.test('reports the per-1k prices and ceilings', () => body.error.input_price === 1 && body.error.output_price === 2 && body.error.max_input === 0.5 && body.error.max_output === 0.5);

request.description: `gpt-4o` is admitted by the consumer model policy but its per-1k price exceeds the cost cap ceiling, so the plugin short-circuits at `pre_request` with `403` and a `{"error":{"type":"model_too_expensive","model":"gpt-4o","input_price":1,"output_price":2,"max_input":0.5,"max_output":0.5}}` body; upstream is never contacted.
Switches the cost cap to `downgrade`: an over-ceiling model is transparently rewritten to `cost_cap.downgrade_to` (which must resolve to the same provider) instead of being rejected.

Same over-ceiling `gpt-4o` request as step 9, but the cost cap is now in `downgrade` mode, so the plugin rewrites the outbound model to `gpt-4o-mini`, emits `X-NeuralTrust-Model-Downgraded: gpt-4o→gpt-4o-mini`, and the request succeeds against OpenAI.

Replaces the cost cap with a cumulative token budget: an aggregate cap of 1 token over a 1-minute window, keyed at consumer scope. The gate checks the consumed counter at `pre_request` and accrues actual provider usage at `post_response`, so the first request always passes and the next one is blocked.
The aggregate counter starts at zero, so this crossing request passes and its real token usage is accrued (well over the 1-token cap), arming the budget for the next call.

exec: pm.test('error type is token_budget_exceeded', () => body.error && body.error.type === 'token_budget_exceeded');

request.description: After step 13 accrued usage past the 1-token cap, the budget gate rejects this request with `429`, the `X-Budget-*` headers describing the breached window, and a structured `{"error":{"type":"token_budget_exceeded",...}}` body; upstream is never contacted.

item.name: Rate limiter
item.description: Exercises the `rate_limiter` plugin at `pre_request`. The policy allows 2 requests per 1m window, counted per consumer. Steps 8-9 stay within the limit (200, with `X-RateLimit-consumer-*` headers). Step 10 exceeds it and asserts the `429` short-circuit with a `Retry-After` header; upstream is never contacted.

request.description: The policy `slug` must equal the plugin catalog name.
First request inside the window: counted and forwarded to OpenAI.
Second (last) allowed request inside the window.
Third request exceeds the 2-per-window limit: the plugin short-circuits with `429` and a `Retry-After` header before reaching upstream.

item.name: Per-tool rate limiter
item.description: Exercises the `per_tool_rate_limiter` plugin at `pre_request` and `pre_response`. The plugin counts real tool executions at `pre_request` from the `role:"tool"` results the client returns on the next request, deduplicated by `tool_call_id`; a tool call is only charged after it is executed, never when the model first requests it. The rule allows 2 executions of `get_weather` per 1m window, scoped per consumer. The model is forced (via `tool_choice`) to call `get_weather` on every request; while under the limit the tool call is forwarded (200) and the execution is charged once the client sends back the `role:"tool"` result. Once the budget is spent, `behavior: reject_response` short-circuits at `pre_request` with `429`, a `tool "get_weather" rate limit exceeded` body, a `Retry-After` and an `X-RateLimit-Tool` header; upstream is never contacted. `inject_error_result` instead removes the over-limit tool call from the response at `pre_response` and injects a `... is rate limited and was not executed` message (200). The third behavior, `strip_tool_from_request`, removes the tool from the outbound request before it reaches upstream. For native MCP `tools/call` traffic the same rule enforces at `pre_request` (deny with JSON-RPC `-32001`, upstream `CallTool` skipped) and counts once at `pre_response` after the call succeeds; on MCP all configured behaviors collapse to deny/block, and `tools/list` never counts. Set `openai_api_key` to a real key and run top-to-bottom.

request.description: The policy `slug` must equal the plugin name `per_tool_rate_limiter`. The plugin enforces and counts at `pre_request` (real executions taken from the returned `role:"tool"` results) and can inject at `pre_response`. The single rule allows 2 executions of `get_weather` per 1m window; `behavior_default: reject_response` returns a `429` once the limit is reached.

`tool_choice` forces the model to call `get_weather`. The counter is below the limit, so the request is forwarded and the tool call reaches the client. The execution is counted at `pre_request` on the following request, once the client returns the `get_weather` `role:"tool"` result.
Another forced `get_weather` call inside the window; each returned execution result is counted at `pre_request`, spending the 2-per-window budget.

exec: pm.test('tool rate limit message', () => pm.response.text().includes('rate limit exceeded'));

request.description: Third request to call `get_weather` exceeds the 2-per-window limit. With `reject_response`, the plugin short-circuits at `pre_request` with `429`, a `tool "get_weather" rate limit exceeded` body, an `X-RateLimit-Tool: get_weather` header and a `Retry-After`; upstream is never contacted.

Switches the same rule to `inject_error_result`. The counter is still over the limit, so instead of rejecting the request, the plugin now lets it reach upstream, then removes the over-limit tool call from the response at `pre_response` and injects an explanatory message in its place.

item.name: 12. Proxy - over limit, tool call injected away -> 200

exec: pm.test('tool call replaced with rate-limit notice', () => pm.response.text().includes('is rate limited and was not executed'));

request.description: Same forced `get_weather` call while still over the limit, but the policy now injects instead of rejecting: the response is forwarded with `200`, the over-limit tool call is dropped and replaced with content like `The tool "get_weather" (call ...) is rate limited and was not executed.`

item.name: Request size limiter
item.description: Exercises the `request_size_limiter` plugin at `pre_request`. The policy caps the body at 1 kilobyte. Step 8 sends a tiny body (200, forwarded to OpenAI). Step 9 sends a body larger than 1 KB and asserts the `413` rejection with a `request size limit exceeded` message; upstream is never contacted.

request.description: The policy `slug` must equal the plugin catalog name.
Body is well under 1 KB, so it passes and is forwarded to OpenAI.
Body exceeds the 1 KB limit, so the plugin short-circuits with `413` before reaching upstream.

exec: pm.test('message mentions size limit', () => pm.response.text().indexOf('request size limit exceeded') !== -1);

item.name: Semantic cache
item.description: Exercises the `semantic_cache` plugin at `pre_request` (lookup) and `post_response` (store). Step 8 sends a question (cache MISS, forwarded to OpenAI; the answer is embedded and stored asynchronously). Step 9 sends the same question after a short wait and asserts a cache HIT (`X-Cache-Status: HIT` plus `X-Cache-Similarity`) served without contacting upstream. Requires a Redis Stack (RediSearch) vector store and a valid `openai_api_key` for embeddings.

request.description: The policy `slug` must equal the plugin catalog name.
First call: no semantically similar entry exists, so it forwards to OpenAI and the answer is stored on `post_response`.

exec: pm.test('first call is not a cache hit', () => pm.response.headers.get('X-Cache-Status') !== 'HIT');

request.description: Identical question after a short wait: the lookup finds the stored answer above the similarity threshold and serves it from cache without contacting upstream.

exec: // Give the async post_response store time to persist the first answer.
